currentState.description: The current public corridor maps synthetic OpenCompliance atomic controls to public-safe family proxy targets instead of reviewed exact ISO 27001, SOC 2, IRAP, or GDPR source anchors.

targetState.description: State of the art is not a one-theorem-per-clause encoding. It is a reviewed mapping layer where each atomic obligation carries exact source anchors, route, relation semantics, method, and confidence before the decidable slice enters Lean 4.
requiredCapabilities: Decompose source standards into atomic obligations before proof assignment.
Attach exact authoritative source anchors such as clauses, Annex A controls, criteria, points of focus, or equivalent framework-native identifiers.
Persist mapping relation, mapping method, review status, and confidence for each mapping edge.
Keep decidable, attestation, judgment, and mixed routes explicit at the atomic-obligation layer.
Export machine-readable OSCAL mapping collections at control, statement, objective, or comparable granularity.
Publish coverage and non-coverage boundaries so consumers can see which source anchors remain unresolved.

boundary.proves: Scoped administrative identities require MFA and conditional access in the declared environment.
doesNotProve: Whole-program access governance adequacy
Periodic access review completion unless a typed export exists

controls.title: Typed periodic access review export present

boundary.proves: That a typed access review export exists for the declared review window.
doesNotProve: Adequacy of reviewer judgment
Effectiveness of each reviewed entitlement decision

boundary.proves: Only that a named human attestation states the declared access review completed for the scoped population and that no high-risk findings remain open in that attested window.
doesNotProve: That each reviewed entitlement decision was correct
That reviewer judgment was sufficient for every edge case

boundary.proves: That the scoped infrastructure access plane requires named identities and has no shared administrative accounts in the declared environment.
doesNotProve: Password, token, or key strength for every identity
Out-of-scope emergency or break-glass account governance
That all access review or least-privilege decisions are otherwise correct

boundary.proves: That the scoped password policy enforces a minimum length of at least 12, has no maximum-length restriction, and blocks common passwords for the declared account slice.
doesNotProve: That every password is unique or uncompromised in practice
That phishing-resistant or passwordless authentication is deployed
That the wider authentication lifecycle is otherwise adequate

boundary.proves: That a managed web application firewall is attached to the scoped public ingress path, runs in blocking mode, and has the managed rule set active in the declared slice.
doesNotProve: That the managed rule set is otherwise adequate for the application's full threat model
That every custom rule, tuning choice, or false-positive handling decision is correct
That downstream application-layer authorization or input-validation controls are otherwise satisfied

boundary.proves: That audit logging is enabled and a retention window is declared in the scoped runtime.
doesNotProve: That logs are reviewed effectively
That the retained log set is sufficient for every incident class

boundary.proves: That the scoped runtime forwards telemetry to the declared central monitoring sink and has alert rules enabled.
doesNotProve: That monitoring coverage is complete for every asset outside the declared scope
That alert tuning or triage quality is adequate for every incident class

boundary.proves: Only that a named human attestation states monitoring review cadence, ownership, and retention operations are current for the declared scope.
doesNotProve: That every alert is analyzed correctly or on time
That the monitoring programme is adequate for every threat scenario

boundary.proves: That public ingress is HTTPS-only, uses managed certificates, and enforces TLS 1.2 or higher.
doesNotProve: That plaintext transport is explicitly disabled rather than merely redirected elsewhere
That the wider network architecture is sufficient
That every downstream connection uses the same cryptographic posture

boundary.proves: That the scoped ingress path traverses the declared managed network boundary.
doesNotProve: That every possible route through the wider estate traverses the same boundary
That the declared ingress topology is sufficient for the whole perimeter architecture

boundary.proves: That administrative ingress is restricted to declared approved source ranges at the scoped managed boundary.
doesNotProve: That the approved source ranges themselves are adequate
That non-administrative exposure is otherwise safe by virtue of this restriction alone

boundary.proves: That plaintext transport is disabled on the scoped public ingress path.
doesNotProve: That every downstream service hop enforces the same transport posture
That cryptographic key management or cipher-suite adequacy is resolved by this control alone

boundary.proves: That the scoped production slice enforces customer and non-production separation and has no undeclared cross-environment paths in that declared boundary.
doesNotProve: That every broader application-layer authorization path is otherwise correct
That every possible data-sharing arrangement outside the declared scope is eliminated
That legal or contractual tenancy boundaries are fully satisfied by this technical predicate alone

boundary.proves: That encryption at rest is enabled for the scoped customer data stores and no unencrypted store is present in the declared slice.
doesNotProve: That key lifecycle, rotation, or separation-of-duty requirements are fully satisfied
That every possible storage location outside the declared scoped data stores is covered

boundary.proves: That the scoped repository default branch is protected with approvals, status checks, and force-push restrictions.
doesNotProve: That every code review is sufficient
That the wider secure development lifecycle is adequate

boundary.proves: That the scoped CI workflow requires review, trusted publishing, protected refs, and environment approvals.
doesNotProve: That every pipeline step is correct
That build and release governance is complete across every repository

decomposition.why: Repository branch protections are the narrow repository-policy slice of the broader change-control boundary.
CI workflow policy is the narrow delivery-policy slice of the broader change-control boundary.
Change-review governance remains signed documentary evidence in the current corridor.

boundary.proves: Nothing directly. This boundary exists to decompose one higher-level change-control area into machine-checkable and attested sub-obligations.
doesNotProve: That the whole secure development lifecycle is adequate
That every specific change approval decision was correct

boundary.proves: That a named human attested the scoped change-review policy is approved and an emergency change path is declared for the current review window.
doesNotProve: That every individual approval followed the declared process
That the wider change-management programme is adequate across every system

boundary.proves: That the scoped runtime service accounts do not rely on user-managed long-lived keys.
doesNotProve: That every secret-management workflow in the organisation is adequate
That human credential handling outside the scoped runtime is sufficient

boundary.proves: That the scoped workload runs only in the declared approved regions and has no undeclared regions.
doesNotProve: That every personal-data transfer mechanism is lawful
That residency or sovereignty adequacy is resolved for every legal context

boundary.proves: That backup snapshots are enabled with a declared schedule and immutable window.
doesNotProve: That restore operations succeed in practice
That recovery objectives are adequate for every dependency

boundary.proves: Only that a restore exercise attestation exists in the declared review window.
doesNotProve: That every restore path is technically successful
That recovery coverage is complete across all assets

boundary.proves: Only that a continuity-plan attestation exists with a declared owner, review window, and scoped critical-service coverage statement.
doesNotProve: That the plan has been tested recently
That recovery objectives or dependency coverage are adequate

boundary.proves: Nothing mechanically. This control is an explicit adequacy boundary for recovery objectives, dependency coverage, and exercised scenario sufficiency.
doesNotProve: That recovery objectives are appropriate
That exercised scenarios cover the material failure modes in scope

boundary.proves: Only that a current scoped risk-management process attestation exists with declared cadence or scope information.
doesNotProve: That the assessment process is adequate
That a scheduled assessment was actually completed

boundary.proves: Only that a scoped risk-management cycle attestation exists for recent assessment, review, treatment, or acceptance activity.
doesNotProve: That the assessed risks were complete
That the follow-through on treatment is sufficient

boundary.proves: Nothing mechanically. This control is an explicit adequacy boundary for periodic risk-assessment cadence, coverage, and follow-through.
doesNotProve: That the risk register is complete
That the assessment cycle is sufficient for the risks in scope

boundary.proves: Only that a human attestation of training completion exists for the declared population and window.
doesNotProve: That the training content is sufficient
That staff behavior actually reflects the training

boundary.proves: Only that a human attestation states the processor register is current and DPA coverage is declared.
doesNotProve: That every processor disclosure obligation is complete
That each vendor control environment is independently sufficient

boundary.proves: Only that a named human attestation states the reviewed high-risk vendors carry the declared security and privacy terms in the current review window.
doesNotProve: That every vendor agreement is complete
That the wider vendor management program is adequate

boundary.proves: Nothing mechanically. This control is an explicit adequacy boundary for the wider vendor management program.
doesNotProve: That vendor tiering is appropriate
That inherited controls and supplier assurance are sufficient

boundary.proves: Only that a named human attestation states an incident response runbook is approved, owned, and current for the declared review window.
doesNotProve: That the incident process is adequate for every scenario
That responders execute the runbook correctly during an incident

boundary.proves: Only that a named human attestation states the incident escalation contacts are current and the escalation path is documented for the declared review window.
doesNotProve: That every contact responds in time
That the wider incident communication program is sufficient

boundary.proves: Only that a DSR runbook attestation exists with a named owner and review window.
doesNotProve: That DSR handling is lawful in every case
That runbook execution times meet every regulatory requirement

controls.title: Privacy by design adequacy review boundary

boundary.proves: Nothing mechanically. This control is an explicit adequacy boundary for human review.
doesNotProve: That privacy by design is satisfied
That product and legal judgment can be reduced to a narrow theorem

controls.title: Data retention and deletion policy attested

boundary.proves: Only that a current retention-and-deletion policy attestation exists for the declared data classes and systems in scope.
doesNotProve: That every retained copy is covered
That deletion behaviour is technically enforced across all holders

boundary.proves: Only that retention schedules, exception handling, and secure deletion procedure attestation exist for the scoped lifecycle surface.
doesNotProve: That schedules are enforced everywhere
That exceptions and downstream holders are fully governed

controls.title: Data retention and deletion adequacy boundary

boundary.proves: Nothing mechanically. This control is an explicit adequacy boundary for retention, deletion, copies, exceptions, and downstream holders of data.
doesNotProve: That every copy is deleted on time
That the scoped retention-and-deletion program is sufficient for all regulatory and operational needs

controls.title: Facility cabling and utility documentation attested

boundary.proves: Only that current documentation exists for the scoped cabling routes, supporting utilities, and dependency points relevant to the systems in scope.
doesNotProve: That the documented routes and utilities are complete for every dependency
That the documented protections are sufficient for all environmental or tampering risks

boundary.proves: Only that protection, maintenance, and contingency measures are attested as current for the scoped cabling routes and supporting utilities.
doesNotProve: That the supporting-utility contingencies have been tested recently
That the protections are sufficient for the actual outage or tampering risks in scope

controls.title: Facility cabling and utility adequacy boundary

boundary.proves: Nothing mechanically. This control is an explicit adequacy boundary for the sufficiency of cabling and supporting-utility safeguards in the scoped environment.
doesNotProve: That the scoped facility and utility safeguards are complete
That the safeguards are sufficient for all environmental, tampering, or availability risks in scope

boundary.proves: Only that current agreement language includes the scoped security commitments required for the customer or third-party relationships in scope.
doesNotProve: That the commitments are monitored everywhere they should be
That the commitments are adequate for the actual service or transfer risks in scope

boundary.proves: Only that ownership, review cadence, and exception handling are attested as current for the scoped security commitments in agreements.
doesNotProve: That reviews are sufficient for every agreement variant
That every exception is justified or controlled adequately

boundary.proves: Nothing mechanically. This control is an explicit adequacy boundary for whether the scoped security commitments in agreements are sufficient for the actual service, transfer, and assurance risks in scope.
doesNotProve: That agreement commitments are complete for every supplier or customer relationship
That the scoped commitments are sufficient for all downstream assurance needs

boundary.proves: Only that a current ISMS context definition attestation exists for the scoped organisation, environment, and boundary assumptions.
doesNotProve: That the context definition is complete
That the scoped assumptions capture every material external or internal factor

boundary.proves: Only that context review, scope adjustment, and relevant factor updates are attested as current for the scoped ISMS.
doesNotProve: That review triggers are sufficient
That the updated context model is still adequate

boundary.proves: Nothing mechanically. This control is an explicit adequacy boundary for whether the scoped ISMS context model is sufficient to frame the organisational, environmental, and boundary assumptions that matter.
doesNotProve: That the context analysis is complete
That the scoped assumptions are sufficient for governance or risk decisions

boundary.proves: Only that a current set of project-management security requirements is attested for the scoped project types and delivery model.
doesNotProve: That the requirements cover every project risk
That the requirements are enforced across every project in scope

boundary.proves: Only that project security review, approval, and exception-handling records are attested as current for the scoped project portfolio.
doesNotProve: That the reviews are sufficiently deep for every project
That the exceptions are justified or controlled adequately

boundary.proves: Nothing mechanically. This control is an explicit adequacy boundary for whether the scoped project-management model integrates security sufficiently throughout the lifecycle in scope.
doesNotProve: That the project-security model covers every lifecycle stage
That the scoped integration is sufficient for all project delivery risks

boundary.proves: Only that a current intake path is attested for the scoped reported security concerns relevant to the service and operations in scope.
doesNotProve: That all reporters know how to use the intake path
That every material concern is reported through it

boundary.proves: Only that tracking, ownership, and resolution-follow-up records are attested as current for the scoped reported security concerns.
doesNotProve: That every concern is resolved on time
That follow-up handling is sufficient for every issue class

boundary.proves: Nothing mechanically. This control is an explicit adequacy boundary for whether the scoped handling model for reported security concerns is sufficient to triage, resolve, and learn from the issues that matter.
doesNotProve: That the scoped issue-handling process is complete
That the handling model is sufficient for every concern type or severity

boundary.proves: Only that a current set of security requirements is attested for the scoped outsourced-development relationships and delivery model.
doesNotProve: That every outsourced development relationship is in scope
That the requirements are sufficient for the actual third-party development risks in scope

boundary.proves: Only that review, oversight, and exception-handling records are attested as current for the scoped outsourced-development relationships.
doesNotProve: That every supplier review is sufficiently deep
That every exception is justified or controlled adequately

boundary.proves: Nothing mechanically. This control is an explicit adequacy boundary for whether the scoped outsourced-development governance model is sufficient to control the third-party development risks that matter.
doesNotProve: That outsourced-development governance is complete
That the scoped oversight model is sufficient for every supplier or delivery path

boundary.proves: Only that a current register or inventory exists for the scoped interested parties and stakeholder groups relevant to the ISMS.
doesNotProve: That every materially relevant stakeholder is captured
That the stakeholder inventory is sufficient for governance or audit needs

boundary.proves: Only that stakeholder expectations, obligations, and review ownership are attested as current for the scoped ISMS surface.
doesNotProve: That every expectation is interpreted correctly
That the stakeholder review model is sufficient for every obligation change

boundary.proves: Nothing mechanically. This control is an explicit adequacy boundary for whether the scoped stakeholder-management model captures the interested parties and expectations that materially affect the ISMS.
doesNotProve: That the stakeholder model is complete
That the scoped obligations and expectations are sufficient for all governance decisions

boundary.proves: Only that a current continual-improvement and corrective-action process is attested for the scoped ISMS surface.
doesNotProve: That the process captures every significant issue
That the process is sufficient to drive meaningful improvement

boundary.proves: Only that nonconformities, corrective-action ownership, and remediation follow-up records are attested as current for the scoped improvement cycle.
doesNotProve: That every corrective action is effective
That remediation follow-up is sufficient for every nonconformity

boundary.proves: Nothing mechanically. This control is an explicit adequacy boundary for whether the scoped continual-improvement and corrective-action loop is sufficient to identify, prioritise, and close meaningful ISMS issues.
doesNotProve: That the improvement loop is complete
That the scoped corrective-action model is sufficient for all issue classes

boundary.proves: Only that a current register of scoped legal, regulatory, contractual, and compliance requirements is attested for the ISMS surface in scope.
doesNotProve: That every materially relevant requirement is captured
That the register is sufficient for every downstream control mapping

boundary.proves: Only that requirement review cadence, update ownership, and change-handling records are attested as current for the scoped compliance inventory.
doesNotProve: That review triggers are sufficient
That every requirement change is reflected everywhere it should be

boundary.proves: Nothing mechanically. This control is an explicit adequacy boundary for whether the scoped compliance-requirement inventory is sufficient to cover the legal, regulatory, and contractual obligations that materially affect the ISMS.
doesNotProve: That the inventory is complete
That the scoped register is sufficient for all audit and governance needs

boundary.proves: Only that a current policy or documented requirement set is attested for the scoped intellectual-property rights obligations affecting the service and workforce in scope.
doesNotProve: That the policy covers every proprietary or third-party rights risk
That the documented obligations are enforced in every relevant workflow

boundary.proves: Only that ownership, issue tracking, and handling rules are attested as current for the scoped intellectual-property rights obligations.
doesNotProve: That every infringement or misuse issue is detected
That the handling model is sufficient for every intellectual-property risk in scope

boundary.proves: Nothing mechanically. This control is an explicit adequacy boundary for whether the scoped intellectual-property posture is sufficient to protect proprietary material and avoid misuse of third-party rights in scope.
doesNotProve: That the intellectual-property posture is complete
That the scoped protections and handling rules are sufficient for every rights scenario

boundary.proves: Only that scoped remote access traverses the declared remote-access path and device posture boundary for the systems in scope.
doesNotProve: That the remote path is technically enforced on every device
That the declared posture boundary is sufficient for every remote-working risk in scope

boundary.proves: Only that a current remote-working procedure is attested for device handling, workspace expectations, and incident reporting for the scoped workforce.
doesNotProve: That every worker follows the procedure
That the procedure is sufficient for the actual remote-working risk in scope

boundary.proves: Nothing mechanically. This control is an explicit adequacy boundary for whether the scoped remote-working boundary, access path, and procedural expectations are sufficient for the systems, data, and workforce risk in scope.
doesNotProve: That the remote-working model is complete
That the scoped remote controls are sufficient for every workforce or data scenario

boundary.proves: Only that a current signed code-of-conduct acknowledgement is attested for the declared workforce population in scope.
doesNotProve: That every individual actually behaves in accordance with the code
That the declared population scope is sufficient for every third-party or workforce risk in scope

boundary.proves: Only that conduct refresh triggers, disciplinary pathways, and exception governance are attested as current for the declared workforce population in scope.
doesNotProve: That the conduct governance model is applied consistently in practice
That the scoped disciplinary pathways are sufficient for every behavioural or security risk in scope

boundary.proves: Nothing mechanically. This control is an explicit adequacy boundary for whether the scoped conduct expectations, refresh triggers, and disciplinary pathways are sufficient for the workforce or contractor risks in scope.
doesNotProve: That the conduct model is complete
That the declared conduct controls are sufficient for every behavioural, access, or security scenario

boundary.proves: Only that a current disciplinary policy or corrective-action rule is attested for the declared workforce populations in scope.
doesNotProve: That the policy is enforced consistently
That the disciplinary model is proportionate or sufficient for every breach scenario

boundary.proves: Only that disciplinary or corrective-action records are attested as current for the declared review window where scoped breaches required them.
doesNotProve: That every relevant breach was detected
That the disciplinary response was timely, consistent, or sufficient

boundary.proves: Nothing mechanically. This control is an explicit adequacy boundary for whether the scoped disciplinary process is credible, timely, and proportionate for the breaches that matter.
doesNotProve: That the disciplinary process is complete
That the scoped corrective-action model is sufficient for every conduct or control breach

boundary.proves: Only that a current internal-audit program and schedule are attested for the scoped control surface.
doesNotProve: That the audit program is independent enough
That the audit schedule covers every materially relevant area

boundary.proves: Only that audit execution records, findings, and tracked follow-up are attested as current for the scoped audit cycle.
doesNotProve: That the audit findings were resolved adequately
That the executed audit work was sufficiently deep or independent

boundary.proves: Nothing mechanically. This control is an explicit adequacy boundary for whether the scoped internal-audit model is independent enough, frequent enough, and broad enough for the assurance risks in scope.
doesNotProve: That the audit model is complete
That the scoped audit coverage is sufficient for every assurance or governance need

boundary.proves: Only that current customer support channels are attested as documented and reachable for the scoped service surface.
doesNotProve: That customers will receive timely or effective support
That the scoped channels are sufficient for every customer or incident scenario

boundary.proves: Only that support staffing, escalation ownership, and response-governance rules are attested as current for the scoped service surface.
doesNotProve: That escalation paths are exercised effectively
That the support governance model is sufficient for the actual service risks in scope

boundary.proves: Nothing mechanically. This control is an explicit adequacy boundary for whether the scoped customer support model provides the right channels, escalation speed, and coverage for the customers and services in scope.
doesNotProve: That the support model is complete
That the scoped channels and escalation paths are sufficient for every customer, availability, or incident scenario

controls.title: Clear desk and screen policy attestation

boundary.proves: Only that a current clear desk and clear screen policy is attested for the scoped workforce and physical work areas.
doesNotProve: That the policy is followed consistently
That the scoped policy is sufficient for every physical disclosure or shoulder-surfing risk

controls.title: Clear desk and screen coverage attestation

boundary.proves: Only that policy communication, acknowledgement, and practical coverage are attested as current for the scoped work areas and actor groups.
doesNotProve: That the communicated controls are practiced consistently
That the declared coverage is sufficient for every physical workspace in scope

controls.title: Clear desk and screen adequacy boundary

boundary.proves: Nothing mechanically. This control is an explicit adequacy boundary for whether the scoped clear desk and screen controls are strong enough for the exposure and shoulder-surfing risks in scope.
doesNotProve: That the workspace-control model is complete
That the scoped desk and screen controls are sufficient for every location or exposure scenario

boundary.proves: Only that current membership or participation records are attested for the scoped external security or special-interest groups.
doesNotProve: That the external groups are relevant enough to the actual threat landscape
That participation is frequent or effective enough to matter operationally

boundary.proves: Only that ownership, review cadence, and dissemination handling are attested as current for the scoped external security inputs.
doesNotProve: That the organization acts on external insights effectively
That the dissemination model is sufficient for every threat, product, or control surface in scope

boundary.proves: Nothing mechanically. This control is an explicit adequacy boundary for whether the scoped external security-community participation is relevant, timely, and broad enough for the threat landscape in scope.
doesNotProve: That the external-intelligence model is complete
That the scoped memberships and follow-up practices are sufficient for every threat, supplier, or customer exposure

boundary.proves: That the scoped network boundary is default-deny inbound and only declared ingress paths remain exposed.
doesNotProve: That every route through the wider network estate is reviewed correctly
That firewall policy alone makes the whole perimeter sufficient

boundary.proves: That a typed baseline configuration export exists for the scoped assets and that prohibited insecure defaults are absent in the current snapshot.
doesNotProve: That every hardening rule is complete for every asset class
That the organization-wide configuration program is otherwise adequate

boundary.proves: Only that a named human attestation states the configuration exception register is current and no declared exception is already past expiry in the review window.
doesNotProve: That every exception is justified
That the wider hardening program is adequate

boundary.proves: That the scoped asset set is on supported versions and that no security update is overdue beyond the declared patch window.
doesNotProve: That vulnerability management is complete for every dependency type
That emergency remediation decisions were always adequate

boundary.proves: Only that a named human attestation states the patch exception register is current, compensating controls are declared, and no exception is already past expiry in the review window.
doesNotProve: That every patch exception is justified
That exception compensating controls are sufficient

boundary.proves: Nothing mechanically. This control is an explicit adequacy boundary for patch cadence, exception thresholds, and compensating controls.
doesNotProve: That the patch program is adequate for asset criticality
That delayed remediation decisions were always appropriate

boundary.proves: That the scoped endpoints have anti-malware or application-control protection enabled and that the protection state is current in the typed export.
doesNotProve: That every endpoint detection rule is tuned correctly
That malware protection alone is sufficient for the broader endpoint-security program

controls.title: AI system context and intended use documented

sourceMappings.framework: UK ICO AI and data protection guidance

targetAnchorPlans.framework: UK ICO AI and data protection guidance

boundary.proves: Only that a signed attestation states the scoped AI system's intended use, deployment context, owner, and affected stakeholder class are documented.
doesNotProve: That the documented context is complete or adequate
That the AI system is lawful or acceptable for every deployment

sourceMappings.framework: UK AI Cyber Security Code of Practice

targetAnchorPlans.framework: UK AI Cyber Security Code of Practice

boundary.proves: Only that a signed attestation states a lifecycle AI risk-management process exists, declared risk tolerances are recorded, and the review window is current.
doesNotProve: That the AI risk-management process is adequate
That every identified risk has been mitigated sufficiently

sourceMappings.framework: UK AI Cyber Security Code of Practice
UK ICO AI and data protection guidance

targetAnchorPlans.framework: UK AI Cyber Security Code of Practice
UK ICO AI and data protection guidance

boundary.proves: Only that a signed attestation states human oversight roles, escalation paths, and override responsibilities are documented for the scoped AI system.
doesNotProve: That the assigned humans will exercise oversight effectively
That the oversight design is sufficient for every use case

controls.title: AI monitoring and incident handling plan documented

sourceMappings.framework: UK AI Cyber Security Code of Practice

targetAnchorPlans.framework: UK AI Cyber Security Code of Practice

boundary.proves: Only that a signed attestation states there is a monitoring owner, an incident escalation path, and a current review window for the scoped AI system.
doesNotProve: That monitoring coverage is adequate
That every incident will be detected or handled correctly

sourceMappings.framework: UK ICO AI and data protection guidance

targetAnchorPlans.framework: UK ICO AI and data protection guidance

boundary.proves: That the scoped generated-content pipeline enforces a declared disclosure marker or metadata flag for outputs in the current configuration snapshot.
doesNotProve: That every user-facing disclosure is understandable in context
That the wider transparency program is complete

boundary.proves: That the scoped generated-content pipeline emits machine-readable provenance metadata or an equivalent origin-history marker for AI-generated or AI-modified outputs in the current configuration snapshot.
doesNotProve: That provenance survives every downstream transformation or repost
That every consumer validates or understands the provenance signal

controls.title: AI evaluation design and findings documented

boundary.proves: Only that a signed attestation states the scoped AI system has documented evaluation scenarios, testing levels, findings capture, and an owner or review window for the recorded results.
doesNotProve: That the evaluation design is sufficiently adversarial, representative, or statistically sound
That the reported findings cover every real-world risk or impact

controls.title: AI data quality and lineage governance documented

boundary.proves: Only that a signed attestation states the scoped AI system has named data sources or categories, lineage notes, quality criteria, and an owner or steward for review.
doesNotProve: That the underlying data is lawful, representative, or free from bias
That the documented data-quality criteria are sufficient for the intended use

boundary.proves: Nothing mechanically. This is an explicit whole-program adequacy judgment claim.
doesNotProve: That any framework is fully satisfied
